text: An incision is made in the right flank for future ileostomy placement. An 11mm trocar is inserted, followed by a 10mm 30-degree rigid scope. A 6mm flexible trocar is introduced at the twelve o'clock position, and another at the six o'clock position. A bi-curved grasping forceps is inserted through the twelve o'clock trocar, while a monocurve grasping forceps is inserted through the six o'clock trocar.

The surgeon places the greater omentum and small bowel above the right liver. A monocurve coagulating hook is inserted at the six o'clock position to identify and dissect the origin of the inferior mesenteric vein. A straight five millimeter clipper plier is used to clip the vein, followed by monocurve scissors to cut it.

The surgeon dissects the left mesocolon, respecting Toldt's fascia, and frees the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin, securing it with 5mm clips before cutting it with monocurve scissors. The rectosigmoid colon is mobilized from medial to lateral, and the sigmoid and left colon are freed from the lateral parietal wall.
The surgeon dissects the girotofascia from the left mesocolon. The left mesocolon is then sectioned intercorporeally using a monocurve coagulating hook and secured with five millimeter clips.

The monocurve needle holder is inserted through the flexible trocar at a six o'clock position. A straight needle is introduced percutaneously above the pelvic bone into the cavity, and the uterus is retracted. The suture is clamped externally using Kelly Graspers.

The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision, identifying and respecting the presacral fascia. The excision begins posteriorly, followed by lateral dissection, mobilizing the left side. The rectum is then mobilized anteriorly while preserving its attachments to the vagina.
The surgeon performs total mesorectal excision (TME) by dissecting downwards and laterally on the left side, then posteriorly while maintaining a position anterior to the presacral fascia.

The levator ani muscle on the left side is visible. A percutaneous suture is placed in the peritoneal sheath covering the vagina, enhancing the exposure of the operative field. Posterior to the rectum, the inferior rectal artery is isolated, clipped, and divided. The total mesorectal excision (TME) is completed posteriorly, respecting both levator ani muscles. The dissection then returns anteriorly, mobilizing the rectum from the vagina and laterally, with the level of the rectal section being chosen.

The surgeon introduces a five millimeter scope into the flexible trocar positioned at six o'clock. An eleven millimeter trocar is replaced by a thirteen millimeter trocar to accommodate the linear stapler. The inferior rectum is then divided using a blue load firing of the linear stapler.

The surgeon grasps and removes the rectum through the single axis. The central and flexible trocars are removed, and the fascia is joined together. A plastic protection is introduced for specimen extraction. The left colon is cut, and the anvil of the circular stapler is introduced. A purse string suture is performed, and the anvil is pushed into the abdominal cavity. The peritoneal sheet and anterior rectifacia are partially closed. The eleven millimetre trocar is reinserted, and the circular stapler is inserted transanally to perform the colorectal mechanical anastomosis under laparoscopic view. A leak test is conducted, and the mesocolic window is closed, starting with the closure of the pelvic peritoneal sheet.

The surgeon continues the suture until reaching the first jejunal loop. The entire small bowel is repositioned into its natural position. The ileocecal valve is identified, and an ileal loop is selected approximately twenty centimeters from the valve. Trochars are removed, and the selected segment of the small bowel is prepared for a temporary ileostomy at the site of the single incision.
